            <command-args></command-args>

Continue interactive editing of README.md, one theme at a time, in conversation with me. We just finished a pass (Windows host-status correction, an Operator Federation roadmap entry with the domain-name prerequisite, a "Bottle Credential Custody" rename, and anchoring the roadmap bullets). That work is committed and pushed — don't redo it. This session targets OTHER places in the README. Orient first: read README.md in full. For context, also read the roadmap adoc (Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc) and skim RBS0-SpecTop.adoc — the README should stay faithful to those. Open a JJK officium at the start. Then DISCUSS before editing: propose which areas you'd sharpen and why, surface tradeoffs, and let me steer. Don't draft prose until we've agreed on a theme. I iterate a lot before things set — circling is how the good version gets found. Working rhythm (important): - Make edits theme-by-theme, then NOTCH FREQUENTLY: jjx_record heat-affiliated against firemark BV (rbk-mvp-uncontrolled-cygwin-test — "close enough" for this README documentation work), with a synthesized intent. Small, frequent commits. - After you notch, I run `git pull --rebase` then `git push` myself (other officia push to main concurrently, so rebase-before-push is the cycle). You don't push. - Never use forbidden git ops (reset/restore/checkout-discard/clean/stash); commit only the README (and any doc you and I explicitly agree to touch). README conventions to match: - Anchors are `` with `[Name](#Name)` self-links; cross-link related sections. Roadmap entries are 2-3 sentences, no more. - Preserve the existing linked-term/glossary style and the role/operation vocab. One carried-over open question you can raise when relevant: whether to widen the public roadmap's curated set by pulling more entries across from RBSHR (hoard, cross-cloud embassy/envoy, reproducibility, etc.) — currently the README keeps a curated eight. Posture: sincerity over polish — tell me hard truths, flag stale or contradictory text, say where you're guessing. Start by orienting and telling me, plainly, what you see worth improving.

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Refine README.md with roadmap alignment review

content.text: I'll start by orienting. Let me load the JJK tool and read the key documents in parallel.

content.content:
1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides two independent container image capabilities: 4 5 - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform container images (x86 + ARM), fetch, retain, and serve them using a role-managed private cloud registry — with optional [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) builds and supply-chain [provenance](#Provenance) 6 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — even using images unmodified from "in the wild" 7 8 > [!IMPORTANT] 9 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** 10 > 11 > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation chain, [build isolation](#BuildIsolation), and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 12 > 13 > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. 14 > 15 > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. 16 > Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 17 18 **Host platform scope.** [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is release-1 qualified on Linux and macOS with Docker. Windows host support works and is exercised in testing, but is not yet part of the release-1 qualification baseline — treat it as supported-experimental for now. 19 20 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is a set of bash scripts enabling enterprise grade container image management intended for incorporation into any project. 21 The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. 22 After initial manual setup, all cloud API calls use `openssl` + `curl`. 23 A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. 24 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. 25 26 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 27 28

29 30

31 32 ## Environment 33 34 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable [provenance](#Provenance), and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. 35 The two compose but neither requires the other. 36 37 ### Supported Platforms 38 39 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime is qualified for release-1 against **Docker** as the container runtime, on two host families: 40 41 - **Linux host** — native Docker Engine (Docker CE / `docker.io`) running directly on the host kernel; no VM. Tested on Ubuntu LTS with cgroup v2 and a 6.x kernel. 42 - **macOS host** — Docker Desktop for Mac on a supported macOS release. Apple Silicon hosts use the Apple Virtualization framework or Docker VMM hypervisor backend. 43 44 Windows host support works — [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) runs on Windows via Docker Desktop — but is not yet part of the release-1 qualification baseline; treat it as supported-experimental pending a green Windows test pass. 45 Podman support is architecturally accommodated by the spec but deferred — see [Podman Support](#PodmanSupport). 46 47 All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. 48 Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRD](#RBRD)), repository-wide settings ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). 49 Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). 50 51 Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. 52 The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. 53 Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands. 54 Each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) is named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece tells you what it does. 55 56 Every state-changing [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) writes three [Log](#Log) files to the directory named by `BURS_LOG_DIR` in your [BURS](#BURS) station file: a stable-name file (always the same path — easy for tooling to locate and evaluate the most recent run), a per-command file (same command name across runs — tools like SlickEdit sense diffs between executions), and a timestamped historical file (permanent record). 57 Disk space is cheap; [Log](#Log) unconditionally so the diagnostic evidence is always there when something fails. 58 Handbooks don't [Log](#Log) — teaching output is ephemeral. 59 60 A [Transcript](#Transcript) is a single file capturing key decision points and state transitions within a [Tabtarget's](#Tabtarget) execution. 61 Where [Logs](#Log) preserve full terminal output, a [Transcript](#Transcript) records the structured progress of sophisticated orchestration commands — the first thing to read when debugging a multi-step failure. 62 63 The [Output](#Output) directory is a fixed-path staging area cleared and recreated before each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) runs. 64 Commands that produce artifacts write them here. 65 Concurrent bash sessions share this path, so parallel commands can overwrite each other's [Output](#Output). 66 67 To begin, run the onboarding walkthrough: 68 69 ``` 70 tt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh 71 ``` 72 73 ## Foundry 74 75 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. 76 The [Foundry](#Foundry) manages [Depot](#Depot) access, [Vessels](#Vessel) choreography, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. 77 Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) ([egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) build from source with [SLSA provenance](#Provenance)), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). 78 Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. 79 80 The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. 81 Builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned [Depot](#Depot) registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. 82 83 ### Depot 84 85 The facility where container images are built and stored — has its own GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket, funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) billing account. 86 The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. 87 Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. 88 89 Each [Depot](#Depot) supports two build egress profiles: 90 91 - **[Tethered](#Tethered)** — Build egress mode allowing public internet access during Cloud Build. [Tethered](#Tethered) builds pull base images from upstream registries at build time — simpler to set up, but dependent on upstream availability. 92 - **[Airgap](#Airgap)** — Build egress mode with no public internet access during Cloud Build. 93 [Airgap](#Airgap) builds draw all dependencies from [Enshrined](#Enshrine) images in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — fully self-contained, independent of upstream availability. 94 Requires [Enshrining](#Enshrine) base images before the first build. 95 See [Build Isolation](#BuildIsolation) for the security rationale behind these profiles. 96 97 ### Manor 98 99 The [Payor's](#Payor) administrative seat — holds the billing account, OAuth client, and operator identity. 100 [Depot](#Depot) projects are created and funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) authority. 101 The [Manor](#Manor) has its own GCP project, distinct from any [Depot](#Depot) project. 102 103 ### Payor 104 105 [Establishes](#Establish) a [Manor](#Manor) and funds [Depot](#Depot) projects through it; authenticates via OAuth. 106 The [Payor](#Payor) is the only role requiring manual Google Cloud Console interaction — [Establishing](#Establish) the [Manor](#Manor), configuring OAuth, and [Installing](#Install) credentials via browser flow. 107 All other roles descend from credentials the [Payor's](#Payor) infrastructure creates. 108 109 ### Governor 110 111 Administers a [Depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access. 112 The [Governor](#Governor) is [Mantled](#Mantle) by the [Payor](#Payor) and holds the administrative credential for the [Depot](#Depot). 113 The [Governor](#Governor) [Invests](#Invest) [Directors](#Director) and [Retrievers](#Retriever) with credentials, [Divests](#Divest) credentials no longer needed, and [Rosters](#Roster) issued credentials. 114 115 ### Director 116 117 Builds and publishes [Vessel](#Vessel) images into a [Depot](#Depot). 118 Each [Director](#Director) credential is scoped to one [Depot](#Depot). 119 The [Director](#Director) manages the image lifecycle: [Ordain](#Ordain) a [Hallmark](#Hallmark), [Tally](#Tally) registry health, [Rekon](#Rekon) raw tags, [Vouch](#Vouch) [provenance](#Provenance), [Abjure](#Abjure) superseded artifacts, and [Jettison](#Jettison) individual tags. 120 121 ### Retriever 122 123 Pulls and runs [Vessel](#Vessel) images from a [Depot](#Depot). 124 This is the most constrained role — read-only access to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. 125 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use, [Plumbs](#Plumb) their [provenance](#Provenance), or [Wrests](#Wrest) a specific image directly. 126 127 ### Vessel 128 129 A specification for a container image — built from source ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrored from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushed from local ([Graft](#Graft)). 130 Each [Vessel](#Vessel) is a directory under `rbmm_moorings/rbmv_vessels/` containing at minimum an `rbrv.env` configuration file; [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) also include a Dockerfile. 131 A fourth mode, [Kludge](#Kludge), builds locally for development without involving the [Depot](#Depot). 132 133 ### Hallmark 134 135 A specific build instance of a [Vessel](#Vessel), identified by timestamp. 136 [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) are the unit of [provenance](#Provenance) tracking — each one records when and how the image was produced. 137 Each [Hallmark](#Hallmark) produces three tagged artifacts in the [Depot](#Depot) registry: the container image (`-image`), the [software bill of materials](#SBOM) ([`-about`](#About)), and the cryptographic attestation ([`-vouch`](#Vouch)). 138 [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) files to pin a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) to specific image versions. 139 140 ### Foundry Lifecycle 141 142 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) uses a role-based security model with four roles, each building on the previous: 143 144 | Role | Authenticates via | Purpose | 145 |------|-------------------|---------| 146 | [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [Governor](#Governor) lifecycle | 147 | [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [Depot](#Depot) | 148 | [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies [provenance](#Provenance) | 149 | [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use | 150 151 The [Payor](#Payor) stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. 152 All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation. 153 Every role's credential is an on-disk key file and **no GCP organization is required**; admitting operators through an external identity provider instead is the planned [Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation) tier. 154 155 #### Establishment and Provisioning 156 157 The [Payor](#Payor) begins by [Establishing](#Establish) a GCP project and configuring an OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. 158 After downloading the OAuth client credentials, the [Payor](#Payor) [Installs](#Install) them via a browser authorization flow — the resulting refresh token is stored locally with restrictive permissions and does not need to be repeated. 159 160 With [Payor](#Payor) credentials in place, the [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), provisioning it with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets storage. 161 The [Payor](#Payor) then [Mantles](#Mantle) a [Governor](#Governor) service account to administer the [Depot](#Depot). 162 163 Before the first build can run, the [Depot](#Depot) needs its supply-chain infrastructure in place: upstream base images must be [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the registry, and a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) of builder tool images must be inscribed. 164 165 #### Credential Distribution 166 167 The [Governor](#Governor) [Invests](#Invest) downstream credentials: a [Director](#Director) for build operations and a [Retriever](#Retriever) for image pull access. 168 Each credential is scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot). 169 170 #### Build and Retrieve 171 172 The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for each [Vessel](#Vessel) — [Conjuring](#Conjure) from source, [Binding](#Bind) from upstream, or [Grafting](#Graft) from local builds. 173 After builds complete, the [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by health status and [Vouches](#Vouch) their [provenance](#Provenance). 174 [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values from the [Tally](#Tally) are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) files, completing the chain from build to runtime. 175 176 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images locally for use. 177 178 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) builds container images on Google Cloud Build (GCB) and stores them in Google Artifact Registry (GAR): 179 180 - Isolated build environments using Google-curated Cloud Build builder images 181 - Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation 182 - [SLSA provenance](#Provenance) attestation and verification 183 - [Software Bills of Material (SBOM)](#SBOM) for every build 184 - Full build transcripts captured as auxiliary metadata artifacts 185 - Upstream base images [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry, so builds do not depend on third-party registry availability at build time 186 - `gcloud` never runs on the workstation — REST calls via `curl` and `jq` drive all remote operations, and the Google-supplied `gcloud` binary is confined to Cloud Build step containers on the server side 187 188 Each build's source context is packaged as a [Pouch](#Pouch) — the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 189 190 ## Crucible 191 192 The distinctive case [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain [provenance](#Provenance). 193 Containers excel at packaging known applications, but running unvetted code poses security risks that ordinary container deployment does not solve. 194 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) assembles a [Crucible](#Crucible) — three cooperating containers where a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces network policy — without requiring modifications to existing container images. 195 The [Bottle](#Bottle) container runs unmodified, in a network namespace prepared by a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle), with all egress flowing through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. 196 197 The [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad running together as one unit defined by a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 198 The [Crucible](#Crucible) is the local safety orchestration — the apparatus that makes running untrusted code practical. 199 [Charging](#Charge) starts all three containers; [Quenching](#Quench) stops and cleans them up. 200 201 ### Nameplate 202 203 Per-[Vessel](#Vessel) configuration tying a [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). 204 The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations. 205 Each [Nameplate](#Nameplate) declares its [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) pins, and the network policy that the [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces. 206 207 ### Containers 208 209 - **[Sentry](#Sentry)** — Security container enforcing network policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq`. 210 The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: DNS-level filtering (only allowed domain names resolve) and IP-level filtering (only allowed CIDR ranges pass). 211 A compromised [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass either layer — the [Sentry](#Sentry) is the sole gateway between the [Bottle](#Bottle) and the outside network. 212 - **[Pentacle](#Pentacle)** — Privileged container establishing the network namespace shared with the [Bottle](#Bottle). 213 The [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runs briefly with elevated privileges to create the network topology, then remains as the namespace anchor. 214 Security policies are enforced from the first packet because the [Sentry](#Sentry) configures the namespace before the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts. 215 - **[Bottle](#Bottle)** — Your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment. 216 The [Bottle](#Bottle) has no direct network access — all traffic routes through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway in a namespace prepared by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle). 217 Any existing container image can run as a [Bottle](#Bottle) without modification. 218 219 ### Enclave 220 221 The isolated network connecting a [Bottle](#Bottle) to its [Sentry](#Sentry) — the [Bottle's](#Bottle) only path to the outside world. 222 All [Bottle](#Bottle) traffic routes through the [Enclave](#Enclave) to the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway; the [Bottle](#Bottle) has no interface on any other network. 223 224 ### Crucible Lifecycle 225 226 [Charge](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) to start the [Sentry](#Sentry), [Pentacle](#Pentacle), and [Bottle](#Bottle) together — the [Bottle](#Bottle) is ready for interactive use immediately. 227 [Rack](#Rack) the [Bottle](#Bottle) to shell in, [Hail](#Hail) the [Sentry](#Sentry) to inspect the gateway, or [Scry](#Scry) the network to observe traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. 228 When finished, [Quench](#Quench) the [Crucible](#Crucible) to stop and clean up all three containers. 229 To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its [provenance](#Provenance) — the full view shows the [SBOM](#SBOM), build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. 230 231 ### Reference Nameplates 232 233 Shipped [Nameplates](#Nameplate) demonstrating different [Crucible](#Crucible) configurations. 234 Each pairs a [Sentry](#Sentry) with a [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) and defines the network policy for that deployment target. 235 236 **[ccyolo](#ccyolo)** — Claude Code sandbox for network-contained AI development. 237 The [ccyolo](#ccyolo) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with a Claude Code [Bottle](#Bottle) under an Anthropic-only network allowlist — SSH entry from the workstation, OAuth authentication via copy/paste, everything else blocked. 238 [Kludge](#Kludge)-only development target: no cloud account, no service account credentials, fully self-contained on the developer's workstation. 239 The onboarding handbook's first hands-on track teaches the full [Crucible](#Crucible) lifecycle using [ccyolo](#ccyolo). 240 241 **[tadmor](#tadmor)** — Adversarial security testing [Nameplate](#Nameplate) for daily iteration. 242 The [tadmor](#tadmor) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack [Vessel](#Vessel) under a restrictive network allowlist, consuming [Kludged](#Kludge) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for fast author-test-iterate cycles. 243 The [Theurge](#Theurge) test orchestrator [Charges](#Charge) [tadmor](#tadmor) and dispatches curated escape attempts to validate that the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment holds under adversarial conditions. 244 245 **[moriah](#moriah)** — Adversarial security testing [Nameplate](#Nameplate) for the airgap supply chain. 246 The [moriah](#moriah) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack [Vessel](#Vessel) under the same restrictive network allowlist as [tadmor](#tadmor), consuming [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) [Ordained](#Ordain) end-to-end on the [Airgap](#Airgap) pool. 247 The [Theurge](#Theurge) runs the same escape attempts against [moriah](#moriah) as against [tadmor](#tadmor) — the cloud-built variant validating that containment holds identically when the supply chain produces the inputs. 248 249 ## Release Procedure 250 251 The project maintainer release qualification ceremony — five operator steps, roughly one hour wall-clock, with cloud cost on the order of two GCP projects per run. 252 See [RELEASE.md](RELEASE.md) for the full procedure. 253 254 ## Appendix: Foundry Operations 255 256 Formal definitions for all [Foundry](#Foundry) operations, organized by lifecycle phase. 257 258 ### Infrastructure 259 260 **[Establish](#Establish)** — Guided setup of a new [Manor](#Manor) — creates the [Manor's](#Manor) GCP project and configures the OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. 261 [Establishing](#Establish) walks the [Payor](#Payor) through project creation, API enablement, and consent screen configuration — the manual prerequisites before any automated operations can run. 262 263 **[Install](#Install)** — Ingest OAuth client credentials from a downloaded JSON key file. 264 [Installing](#Install) triggers a browser authorization flow and stores the resulting refresh token locally with restrictive permissions (`600`). 265 This is a one-time [Payor](#Payor) operation — the refresh token persists until explicitly revoked. 266 267 **[Levy](#Levy)** — Provision a new [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure. 268 [Levying](#Levy) creates the GCP project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and build configuration. 269 This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that binds [Regime](#Regime) configuration to real cloud resources. 270 271 **[Unmake](#Unmake)** — Permanently destroy a [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure — project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and all contents. 272 [Unmaking](#Unmake) is the reverse of [Levying](#Levy). 273 This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation and is irreversible. 274 275 **[Refresh](#Refresh)** — Refresh an expired [Payor](#Payor) OAuth token. 276 OAuth tokens expire periodically; [Refreshing](#Refresh) re-authenticates via the stored refresh token without repeating the full [Install](#Install) flow. 277 Run when [Payor](#Payor) operations fail with authentication errors. 278 279 **[Quota](#Quota)** — Review Cloud Build capacity and usage. 280 [Quota](#Quota) displays the current build minute allocation, consumption, and any throttling in effect for the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project. 281 282 ### Credentials 283 284 **[Mantle](#Mantle)** — Create or replace the [Governor](#Governor) service account for a [Depot](#Depot). 285 [Mantling](#Mantle) is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that provisions the administrative credential — the [Governor](#Governor) inherits the [Payor's](#Payor) authority to manage the [Depot](#Depot) but authenticates via service account key rather than OAuth. 286 287 **[Invest](#Invest)** — Create a [Director](#Director) or [Retriever](#Retriever) service account. 288 [Investing](#Invest) provisions a new credential scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot) and emits its credential file in one step. 289 This is a [Governor](#Governor) operation; investing fails if the named credential already exists, so re-keying requires [Divesting](#Divest) first. 290 291 **[Divest](#Divest)** — Revoke a [Director](#Director) or [Retriever](#Retriever) service account. 292 [Divesting](#Divest) deletes the service account and removes the local credential file — the credential becomes permanently unusable. 293 This is a [Governor](#Governor) operation used when a credential is compromised, no longer needed, or being rotated. 294 295 **[Roster](#Roster)** — Inventory [Director](#Director) or [Retriever](#Retriever) service accounts within a [Depot](#Depot). 296 [Rostering](#Roster) shows credentials issued under each role with their creation dates and status. 297 This is a [Governor](#Governor) operation; observation-only, no cloud mutation. 298 299 ### Supply Chain 300 301 **[Enshrine](#Enshrine)** — Mirror upstream base images into your [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 302 [Enshrining](#Enshrine) ensures the build pipeline has a fixed, self-contained supply chain — builds draw from project-owned copies rather than depending on third-party registry availability at build time. 303 304 **[Reliquary](#Reliquary)** — Co-versioned set of builder tool images (skopeo, docker, gcloud, syft) inscribed from upstream into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 305 Cloud Build jobs use [Reliquary](#Reliquary) images as step containers, ensuring builds run with known, project-owned toolchains rather than pulling tools from upstream at build time. 306 The [Director](#Director) inscribes a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) before any [Ordain](#Ordain) or [Enshrine](#Enshrine) operation can run. 307 308 ### Building 309 310 **[Ordain](#Ordain)** — Create a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) with full attestation — the production build operation. 311 [Ordaining](#Ordain) is mode-aware: it [Conjures](#Conjure), [Binds](#Bind), or [Grafts](#Graft) depending on the [Vessel's](#Vessel) configuration. 312 Each [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an image in the [Depot](#Depot) registry with associated [provenance](#Provenance) metadata. 313 314 **[Conjure](#Conjure)** — Cloud Build creates the image from source. 315 [Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation and [SBOMs](#SBOM). 316 This is the highest-trust build mode. 317 318 **[Bind](#Bind)** — Mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. 319 [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 320 Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build [provenance](#Provenance). 321 322 **[Graft](#Graft)** — Push a locally-built image to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. 323 [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though [About](#About) and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. 324 This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). 325 326 **[Kludge](#Kludge)** — Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without [Depot](#Depot) registry push. 327 [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). 328 The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. 329 330 **[Pouch](#Pouch)** — Build context packaged as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image and pushed to the [Depot's](#Depot) registry before a Cloud Build job runs. 331 The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. 332 This is the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 333 334 ### Verification 335 336 **[Tally](#Tally)** — Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the [Depot](#Depot) registry by health status. 337 [Tallying](#Tally) shows which builds succeeded, which are pending, and which failed. 338 The [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) before [Vouching](#Vouch) to confirm build completion. 339 340 **[Rekon](#Rekon)** — Raw listing of image tags in the [Depot](#Depot) registry for a [Vessel](#Vessel) package. 341 [Rekon](#Rekon) is a [Director](#Director)-only diagnostic that shows exactly what exists in the registry without health interpretation. 342 Where [Tally](#Tally) groups [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by status, [Rekon](#Rekon) shows the unprocessed tag inventory. 343 344 **[Vouch](#Vouch)** — Cryptographic attestation proving a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) was built by trusted infrastructure. 345 The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full [SLSA provenance](#Provenance) verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no [provenance](#Provenance) chain. 346 The [Director](#Director) [Vouches](#Vouch) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) after [Tallying](#Tally) their build status. 347 348 **[About](#About)** — Build metadata and [software bill of materials](#SBOM) for a [Hallmark](#Hallmark). 349 The [About](#About) artifact (`-about` tag) contains the [SBOM](#SBOM), build transcript, build configuration snapshot, and key package summaries — bundled as a compressed archive and stored as a Generic Artifact in GAR. 350 Every [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an [About](#About) alongside the image. 351 352 **[Plumb](#Plumb)** — Inspect an artifact's [provenance](#Provenance) — [SBOM](#SBOM), build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain. 353 [Plumbing](#Plumb) provides full transparency into how an image was built and what it contains. 354 Two views are available: full ([SBOM](#SBOM), build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). 355 356 ### Distribution 357 358 **[Summon](#Summon)** — Pull a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [Depot](#Depot) to your local machine. 359 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use — the final step before a [Hallmark's](#Hallmark) image can be used in a [Crucible](#Crucible). 360 361 **[Wrest](#Wrest)** — Pull a specific image from the [Depot](#Depot) registry by reference. 362 [Wresting](#Wrest) is a direct pull without [Vouch](#Vouch) verification — used when you need a specific image tag regardless of attestation status. 363 Compare with [Summon](#Summon), which enforces the [Vouch](#Vouch) ceremony. 364 365 ### Removal 366 367 **[Abjure](#Abjure)** — Remove a [Hallmark's](#Hallmark) artifacts from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — the `-image`, [`-about`](#About), and [`-vouch`](#Vouch) tags deleted as a coherent unit. 368 [Abjuring](#Abjure) is the reverse of [Ordaining](#Ordain): it formally renounces a build instance. 369 The [Director](#Director) [Abjures](#Abjure) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) that are superseded, broken, or no longer needed. 370 371 **[Jettison](#Jettison)** — Delete a specific image tag from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 372 [Jettisoning](#Jettison) is lower-level than [Abjure](#Abjure) — it removes a single tag rather than a complete [Hallmark](#Hallmark) artifact set. 373 Used for cleanup of individual registry entries. 374 375 ### Diagnostics 376 377 **[List Depots](#ListDepots)** — Inventory all active [Depots](#Depot) visible to the current [Payor](#Payor) credentials. 378 Shows project IDs, regions, and provisioning status. 379 380 **[JWT Probe](#JWTProbe)** — Test service account authentication. 381 The [JWT Probe](#JWTProbe) verifies that a [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) credential can successfully authenticate to the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project — useful for diagnosing access failures after credential creation or rotation. 382 383 **[OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe)** — Test [Payor](#Payor) OAuth authentication. 384 The [OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe) verifies that the stored refresh token can obtain a valid access token — useful for diagnosing [Payor](#Payor) operation failures before attempting a full [Refresh](#Refresh). 385 386 ## Appendix: Crucible Operations 387 388 Formal definitions for all [Crucible](#Crucible) operations. 389 390 ### Lifecycle 391 392 **[Charge](#Charge)** — Start a [Crucible](#Crucible) — the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad — defined by a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 393 [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. 394 395 **[Quench](#Quench)** — Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Crucible's](#Crucible) containers. 396 [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). 397 398 ### Interaction 399 400 **[Rack](#Rack)** — Shell into a [Bottle](#Bottle) container. 401 [Racking](#Rack) opens an interactive session inside the running workload — for debugging, inspecting state, or running commands as the [Bottle](#Bottle) user would experience them. 402 403 **[Hail](#Hail)** — Shell into a [Sentry](#Sentry) container. 404 [Hailing](#Hail) opens an interactive session on the gateway — for inspecting `iptables` rules, `dnsmasq` configuration, network state, and egress logs. 405 406 **[Scry](#Scry)** — Observe network traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. 407 [Scrying](#Scry) captures packets on the [Crucible's](#Crucible) network interfaces — for verifying that blocked traffic is actually blocked, diagnosing connectivity issues, or watching the [Sentry's](#Sentry) filtering in action. 408 409 ## Appendix: Adversarial Test Method 410 411 The [Crucible's](#Crucible) containment is validated through coordinated escape testing using two components: 412 413 - **[Ifrit](#Ifrit)** — Adversarial attack [Vessel](#Vessel) purpose-built to run inside a [Bottle](#Bottle), seeking escape. 414 The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) carries scapy (arbitrary packet construction), strace (syscall boundary probing), and a minimal footprint — tools chosen to probe every surface the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment exposes. 415 Named for the djinn imprisoned in a bottle. 416 - **[Theurge](#Theurge)** — Test orchestrator running on the host, outside the [Crucible](#Crucible). 417 The [Theurge](#Theurge) [Charges](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) as its [Bottle](#Bottle), then dispatches curated, reproducible, version-controlled attack scripts targeting specific surfaces: DNS exfiltration, ICMP covert channels, cloud metadata probing, namespace breakout, and direct IP bypass attempts. 418 Each attack runs inside the [Bottle](#Bottle) while the [Theurge](#Theurge) simultaneously observes the [Sentry's](#Sentry) network from outside — confirming that blocked traffic is actually blocked, not merely unrequested. 419 420 The escape tests were developed through adversarial Claude Code sessions with full visibility into the [Sentry's](#Sentry) source, configuration, and the [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) specification. 421 The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) [Vessel](#Vessel) is the delivery vehicle; the intelligence came from the authoring process. 422 Every test that passes is evidence the containment holds — not proof. 423 The test suite grows as new attack surfaces are identified. 424 425 ## Appendix: Supply Chain Provenance 426 427 Supply chain provenance is a cryptographically signed record of how a container image was produced — what source, what builder, what steps — so that consumers can verify an image came from trusted infrastructure and was not tampered with in transit or at rest. 428 429 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) achieves [SLSA](https://slsa.dev) v1.0 Build Level 3 for [Conjure](#Conjure) builds, auto-generated by Google Cloud Build. 430 The [Vouch](#Vouch) step independently verifies each build's DSSE envelope signature against Google's attestor public keys from `projects/verified-builder` KMS — using Python standard library and `openssl` only, with no third-party verifier. 431 432 Provenance guarantees are mode-aware: 433 434 | [Vessel](#Vessel) Mode | Trust Basis | [Vouch](#Vouch) Verdict | 435 |------|-------------|------| 436 | [**Conjure**](#Conjure) | Full SLSA v1.0 Level 3 — signed build provenance from GCB | DSSE envelope signature verification | 437 | [**Bind**](#Bind) | Digest-pin comparison — image in GAR matches pinned upstream reference | Digest-pin match | 438 | [**Graft**](#Graft) | Locally built and pushed — no cloud build involvement | GRAFTED (explicit no-provenance marker) | 439 440 Deliberately excluded: no `slsa-verifier` binary, no `gcloud` CLI on the workstation, no `jq` in the verification path. 441 The [Vouch](#Vouch) verifier reconstructs Pre-Authenticated Encoding (PAE), decodes the base64url payload and signature, and verifies via `openssl dgst` against embedded attestor keys — a minimal, auditable trust chain. 442 443 ## Appendix: Software Bill of Materials 444 445 A Software Bill of Materials ([SBOM](#SBOM)) is a machine-readable inventory of every component inside a container image — every OS package, every library, every binary, with versions. 446 Without one, a container image is an opaque filesystem whose contents you discover by running it, which is exactly the wrong time to find out it ships a vulnerable dependency. 447 448 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) generates an [SBOM](#SBOM) for every build using [Syft](https://github.com/anchore/syft), scanning each per-platform image during the [About](#About) assembly step. 449 Each architecture gets its own [SBOM](#SBOM), bundled alongside the build transcript and configuration snapshot in the `-about` artifact stored as a Generic Artifact in GAR. 450 451 An [SBOM](#SBOM) enables three hygiene practices that opaque images cannot support: 452 453 - **CVE triage before deployment** — when a vulnerability is announced, search your [SBOMs](#SBOM) rather than scanning running containers 454 - **Pre-deployment audit** — know what you are granting network access to before a [Crucible](#Crucible) is [Charged](#Charge) 455 - **Build-over-build drift detection** — compare [SBOMs](#SBOM) across [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) to see what changed between builds 456 457 The [Plumb](#Plumb) command surfaces [SBOM](#SBOM) contents: the full view shows package inventories; the compact view summarizes key components. 458 459 ## Appendix: Build Isolation 460 461 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) supports two build egress profiles — [Tethered](#Tethered) and [Airgap](#Airgap) — that determine whether a Cloud Build job can reach the public internet. 462 The distinction is not primarily about availability; it is a security boundary that controls what can enter and exit the build environment. 463 464 **What [Airgap](#Airgap) protects: exfiltration and supply chain injection.** 465 If a compromised dependency, build plugin, or Dockerfile instruction executes during your build, an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build cannot phone home — it cannot transmit source code, secrets, or intermediate artifacts to an external endpoint, and it cannot silently fetch malicious payloads. 466 This is defense-in-depth for proprietary code: even if a build step is compromised, the network is not available as an exfiltration channel. 467 468 **The curated gate principle.** 469 [Airgap](#Airgap) does not mean "nothing external." It means all external content enters through a single auditable gate — the [Enshrine](#Enshrine) ceremony — rather than ad-hoc network fetches during build. 470 The attack surface collapses from "any URL a Dockerfile mentions" to "the specific digests the [Director](#Director) [Enshrined](#Enshrine)." 471 Builder tool images enter through a parallel gate: the [Reliquary](#Reliquary), inscribed once and pinned by digest for all subsequent builds. 472 473 **What [Airgap](#Airgap) does not protect: the base image contents.** 474 Base images like `debian-slim` were themselves built with full internet access — `apt-get install` already ran inside them. 475 The [Airgap](#Airgap) protects *your* build steps on top of those bases, not the base image contents themselves. 476 Base images are vetted separately: digest-pinned at [Enshrine](#Enshrine) time, inspectable via [SBOM](#SBOM), and stored as project-owned copies in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 477 A [Tethered](#Tethered) build of the base image followed by an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build of your application is the expected pattern — the base image is a known input, your proprietary layers are the protected output. 478 479 **Regulatory alignment.** 480 No framework mandates build-time network blocking by name, but egress-locked builds are the simplest way to evidence several common controls: FedRAMP CM-7 (least functionality) and SC-7 (boundary protection), SOC 2 CC6.1 (logical access) and CC8.1 (change management), and SLSA Level 3's hermetic build requirement. 481 482 ## Appendix: Roadmap 483 484 The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: 485 486 - **[Crucible Conduit for Cloud Services](#CrucibleConduit)** - Encrypted tunnel from the [Sentry](#Sentry) to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints, enabling [Bottles](#Bottle) to reach cloud AI services (AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI) without exposing floating cloud IP ranges in the CIDR allowlist. 487 WireGuard terminated at the [Sentry](#Sentry) replaces per-service IP tracking with a single stable VPC CIDR. 488 Near-term, allowlist-only [Nameplates](#Nameplate) targeting specific service CIDRs and domains work today with existing [Sentry](#Sentry) machinery. 489 The tunnel adds defense-in-depth for PrivateLink-capable services; SaaS endpoints without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com) remain served by CIDR/domain allowlisting. 490 491 - **[Bottle Credential Custody](#BottleCredentialCustody)** - Move the service secrets a [Bottle](#Bottle) workload uses (cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) off the operator's workstation and into the [Bottle](#Bottle) itself, injected at [Charge](#Charge) time via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration — never baked into the image. 492 The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds the secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge, which blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation. 493 Orthogonal to but paired with the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit): the conduit gives the [Bottle](#Bottle) network reach to a cloud service while the [Sentry](#Sentry) holds the tunnel key, and this holds the service secret inside the [Bottle](#Bottle). 494 495 - **[Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation)** - Admit operators through an external identity provider (OIDC/SAML) instead of distributing service-account key files — identity proved by a fresh sign-in, capabilities granted to a federated principal, no secret on disk. 496 This tier **requires a GCP organization**, which is the dividing line from today's keyfile model (which needs none): a qualifying organization is free via Google Cloud Identity once you verify ownership of a **DNS domain**, so the real prerequisite is controlling a domain, not paying Google. 497 Revocation becomes central at the identity provider rather than per-keyfile rotation. 498 499 - **[VPC Service Controls](#VpcServiceControls)** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. 500 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 501 If a VPC is stood up for the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit) architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers. 502 503 - **[Cosign Container Signing](#CosignSigning)** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. 504 Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. 505 506 - **[CDN-Aware IP Gating](#CdnAwareIpGating)** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. 507 A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. 508 509 - **[Podman Support](#PodmanSupport)** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. 510 On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. 511 Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. 512 513 - **[Crucible-to-Crucible Networking](#CrucibleToCrucible)** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). 514 The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case. 515 516 ## Appendix: Reference Project 517 518 This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle). 519 The annotated tree below └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Bind](#Bind) mode, digest reference | 573 | ` ├── rbev-bottle-anthropic-jupyter/` | [Conjure](#Conjure) — Jupyter notebook server | 574 | ` │ ├── Dockerfile` | | 575 | ` │ └── rbrv.env` | [RBRV](#RBRV) — [Conjure](#Conjure) mode | 576 | ` └── (4 additional test vessels)` | busybox variants for [Theurge](#Theurge) fixture coverage | 577 578 ## Appendix: Specific Regimes 579 580 **[BURC](#BURC)** — Project structure configuration, in the repo. 581 [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) directory, tools directory. 582 583 **[BURS](#BURS)** — Developer workstation configuration. 584 Not in git. 585 Log directory, station paths. 586 587 **[RBRR](#RBRR)** — Repository-wide configuration: runtime container prefix, vessel directory, container DNS, Cloud Build timeouts, secrets directory, public docs URL. 588 589 **[RBRD](#RBRD)** — [Depot](#Depot) identity — cloud prefix, depot moniker, GCP region, Cloud Build pool machine type — populated during [Levy](#Levy) and frozen for the depot's productive lifetime; changing any field after [Levy](#Levy) requires a fresh [Depot](#Depot). 590 591 **[RBRP](#RBRP)** — [Manor](#Manor) identity — billing account, OAuth client ID, operator email, and the [Manor's](#Manor) GCP project. 592 In the repo. 593 594 **[RBRO](#RBRO)** — [Payor](#Payor) OAuth credentials — client secret and refresh token. 595 Not in the repo. 596 597 **[RBRA](#RBRA)** — Role credentials resident on user workstation, enabling [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) operations. 598 One credential file per role per [Depot](#Depot). 599 600 **[RBRV](#RBRV)** — [Vessel](#Vessel) configuration specifying [Bind](#Bind), [Conjure](#Conjure), or [Graft](#Graft) mode for creating [Hallmarks](#Hallmark). 601 602 **[RBRN](#RBRN)** — Per-[Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Crucible](#Crucible) configuration mapping two [Vessels](#Vessel) — [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) — with runtime and [Hallmark](#Hallmark) assignments. 603 604 ## License 605 606 Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. 607 608 Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0. 609
