aiTitle: Refine README.md with roadmap alignment review

content.text:
Yes — there are very canonical approaches, and they split almost exactly along your two edges. That's a gift for the frontmatter: the cost you're naming is *recognizable*.

## What a midsize company actually runs

**Edge 1 — what internal tools can reach (egress).** Two tiers, depending on who's doing it:

- *Corporate-network tier:* a **Secure Web Gateway** (Zscaler, Cisco Umbrella, Netskope, Palo Alto, Cloudflare Gateway) — a full proxy that inspects and filters all outbound traffic against URL/domain policy — usually bundled today into **SASE** (which merges SWG + **CASB** for cloud-app control + firewall + ZTNA into one managed subscription).
- *Container/cluster tier:* **Kubernetes NetworkPolicy** for deny-by-default egress, almost always upgraded to **Cilium** (CiliumNetworkPolicy, DNS-aware egress rules, an **egress gateway**) or Calico/Tigera, or service-mesh egress gateways.

The recurring phrase in the literature — *"deny-by-default egress, allow only where needed"* — is **literally your Sentry model.** The difference is the enterprise gets there by standing up a cluster + a CNI + policy authorship, or by paying for a SASE subscription.

**Edge 2 — where images came from (provenance).** The stabilized stack is **Sigstore/cosign** (signing, keyless via OIDC), **SLSA** provenance, **in-toto** attestations, **SBOM** via Syft, scanning (Trivy/Grype/Snyk/Aqua), and admission control (**Kyverno**, OPA Gatekeeper, Sigstore Policy Controller) to reject unsigned images at deploy. Note: the sources say SLSA *Level 2* is "achievable in weeks" with that toolchain — and Recipe Bottle reaches **Level 3**, the harder tier, without any of it on the workstation.

## My read on how to use this

1. **Name categories, not vendors.** "Secure web gateway, network policy, an admission controller" ages well and still triggers recognition. Vendor names (Zscaler, Cilium) date fast and tilt the tone toward marketing or competitor-knocking. The two safe-to-name-by-name items are the *vendor-neutral standards you actually meet* — **SLSA** and **Sigstore** — because citing them is aspirational, not competitive.
2. **The real cost lever is the team, not the license.** Your instinct is right: the durable claim isn't "this software is expensive," it's "all of this is *standing operational work* — a cluster to run, policy to author, a subscription to administer — i.e., a platform/DevOps function to keep it alive." That's what RB removes.
3. **One hard truth to respect.** RB is *not* a like-for-like replacement for fleet-scale SWG/Cilium — those are multi-tenant, centrally-managed corporate infrastructure. The honest framing is **"the control a funded platform org gets, put in reach of a small team or a solo developer"** — not "RB beats enterprise tooling." If we imply equivalence, the informed reader who runs Cilium bounces.

## A sharpened candidate (to react to)

> Getting that control to hold at the edges is normally a standing job for a platform team — a Kubernetes cluster with network policies, or a secure web gateway subscription, to govern what reaches the internet; a signing-and-attestation pipeline ([SLSA](#Provenance), Sigstore, admission control) to govern where images come from. Recipe Bottle gives a small team that same control for the cost of a few bash scripts.

Dials: how concrete to go (this names the *categories* — we could add one parenthetical vendor for recognition, or strip to "managed tooling and the people to run it"); and whether to foreground the **team/standing-cost** angle harder than the tooling list, since that's your strongest and most durable point. Which way do you want to lean?

**Sources:**
- [Palo Alto — What Is a Secure Web Gateway (SWG)?](https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cyberpedia/what-is-secure-web-gateway)
- [Palo Alto — Secure Web Gateway vs. CASB](https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cyberpedia/swg-vs-casb)
- [Check Point — What Is SASE?](https://www.checkpoint.com/cyber-hub/network-security/what-is-secure-access-service-edge-sase/)
- [Kubernetes — Network Policies](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/network-policies/)
- [Cilium — Egress Gateway](https://cilium.io/use-cases/egress-gateway/)
- [Datadog — deny-by-default network egress at scale](https://www.datadoghq.com/blog/cnm-kubernetes-egress/)
- [Elysiate — Software Supply Chain Security: SBOM, SLSA, Sigstore (2025)](https://www.elysiate.com/blog/supply-chain-security-sbom-slsa-sigstore-2025)
- [AquilaX — Supply Chain Security Beyond SBOMs: Sigstore, SLSA, Build Provenance](https://aquilax.ai/blog/supply-chain-artifact-signing-slsa)

content.text: Yes, that makes complete sense — and it's the right instinct. The opener's only job is the hook; the tooling survey is *credibility ballast*. Ballast belongs low and out of the way, not in the first breath. If sentence two starts enumerating SWG/Cilium/Sigstore, you've buried the hook under exactly the kind of list we were trying to escape. So: crisp words up top, the research one click down. There are two ways to do "under links," and I'd steer you to the second: **(a) Inline external links on the category words** — keep prose tight but make "[secure web gateway](…)", "[network policies](…)" clickable. Lightest touch, but it sends the reader *off your page* in the second sentence, before they're hooked. Bad place to hand someone an exit. **(b) A dedicated anchored section that holds the whole survey** — the opener gestures in crisp prose and carries *one* self-link into it. The reader stays on-page; the research gets room to breathe; and — importantly — the honest caveat ("RB is small-team scale, not a fleet-scale Cilium/SASE replacement") has a natural home down there instead of muddying the hook. The external vendor/standard links live in that section, where clicking away is fine because they've already read the pitch. This also fits the house style: the README already self-links with `` anchors everywhere and reserves external links for genuine outside refs. (b) uses both exactly as intended. Here's the shape — crisp opener, single link: > # Recipe Bottle > > Containers give you control over what runs. Getting that control to hold at the *edges* — knowing where an image actually came from, and constraining what it can reach once it runs — is normally a standing job for a [platform team](#ConventionalStack). > > [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) collapses that cost. It's a set of bash scripts — `bash 3.2`, `curl`, `openssl`, `jq`, and a handful of standard tools, no Python runtime, no language package manager, no `gcloud` CLI — that a small team can use to stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without one. …and the link lands here, where the research survives intact: > ## Appendix: How This Is Normally Done > > *Edge 1 — what code can reach:* secure web gateways / SASE (Zscaler, Netskope, Cloudflare Gateway) at the network tier; Kubernetes NetworkPolicy, Cilium egress gateways, Calico at the cluster tier — all converging on "deny-by-default egress," which is exactly the [Sentry](#Sentry) model. > *Edge 2 — where images came from:* [SLSA](#Provenance) provenance, Sigstore/cosign signing, SBOMs, admission control (Kyverno, OPA Gatekeeper). > *Honest scope:* these are multi-tenant corporate infrastructure run by a standing team. Recipe Bottle puts the same controls in reach of a small team — it does not replace fleet-scale tooling. (External source links attach to the named items down there.) Two things for you to decide: 1. **Placement** — does the survey sit as an end-**appendix** (alongside Provenance, SBOM, Build Isolation), or as a short near-top **"why" section** the reader hits early? Appendix keeps the top crisp; near-top makes the cost concrete sooner. 2. **The section name** — `How This Is Normally Done`, `What Recipe Bottle Stands In For`, `The Conventional Stack`, `Prior Art`? Your call; I'm not attached. Which placement feels right, and does the opener-plus-link division land the way you pictured "under links"?

1. Appendix
2. I do like "How this is normally done".

Do you recommend folding these both under the responsibility of a "Platform Team"?  I do wonder about having discrete links for 'controlled container builds' and 'restricting access'.  Thoughts?

content.text: Both, actually — and I don't think they're in tension once you separate *frame* from *link*. **Fold under "platform team" — yes, as the framing.** Your strongest, most durable point is that the real cost is the standing human function, not the license. "Platform team" names exactly that, and it's faithful: at midsize scale, one platform / DevSecOps function genuinely owns *both* supply-chain security and network policy — they're not two separate teams until you get quite large. So keeping "platform team" as the unifying phrase keeps the headline cost (the people you'd otherwise staff) front and center. That's the thing RB removes. **Discrete links — also yes.** The README's whole spine is the two edges, and the two edges map to genuinely *different ecosystems* (SLSA/Sigstore/admission-control vs SWG/SASE/Cilium). Two links reinforce that spine and let a reader who cares about only one edge jump precisely. A single combined link makes them filter. The reconciliation: **"platform team" stays plain prose (the frame); the two discrete links hang off the two edge-phrases that are *already in the sentence*** — so you get discreteness without adding asides that re-clutter the crisp opener. > Containers give you control over what runs. Getting that control to hold at the *edges* — [knowing where an image actually came from](#ControlledContainerBuilds), and [constraining what it can reach once it runs](#RestrictingAccess) — is normally a standing job for a platform team. …landing in one appendix (your name, your placement) with two sub-anchors carrying your labels: > ## Appendix: How This Is Normally Done > ### Controlled container builds > *(SLSA provenance, Sigstore/cosign, SBOMs, admission control…)* > ### Restricting access > *(secure web gateways / SASE, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy, Cilium — all converging on deny-by-default egress, which is the [Sentry](#Sentry) model)* > *(+ the honest-scope note: corporate infrastructure run by a standing team; RB puts the same controls in reach of a small team, it doesn't replace fleet-scale tooling)* One honest footnote on the frame: at large-enterprise scale, egress (a NetSec team) and supply chain (a platform team) sometimes split. "Platform team" is a small-team-reader's-eye abstraction — true enough for the audience, slightly blurred at the top end. I don't think it's worth caveating in the prose; just so we're not kidding ourselves. The one micro-decision left to you: **link text.** I put the links on the in-sentence phrases ("where it came from" / "what it can reach") because it keeps the opener cleanest. The alternative is surfacing your literal labels — "[controlled container builds](#…)" / "[restricting access](#…)" — as the visible link text, which reads better if you'd rather the links sit on or beside the two edge-bullets than in the opening sentence. Which do you want?

input.old_string:
[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides two independent container image capabilities:

- **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform container images (x86 + ARM), fetch, retain, and serve them using a role-managed private cloud registry — with optional [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) builds and supply-chain [provenance](#Provenance)
- **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — even using images unmodified from "in the wild"
input.new_string:
Containers give you control over what runs. Getting that control to hold at the *edges* — [knowing where an image actually came from](#ControlledContainerBuilds), and [constraining what it can reach once it runs](#RestrictingAccess) — is normally a standing job for a platform team.

[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) collapses that cost. It's a set of bash scripts — `bash 3.2`, `curl`, `openssl`, `jq`, and a handful of standard tools, with no Python runtime, no language package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI — that a small team can use to stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without one. After initial setup, every cloud API call is `openssl` + `curl`.

It extends a container's control to its two edges:

- **[Foundry](#Foundry)** — *where images come from.* Orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform images (x86 + ARM) with verifiable [SLSA provenance](#Provenance), serves them from a role-managed private cloud registry, and can build [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) so a compromised build step cannot phone home.
- **[Crucible](#Crucible)** — *what images can reach.* Runs untrusted containers — even images pulled unmodified "in the wild" — behind enforced network isolation: DNS and IP filtering that a compromised workload cannot bypass.

The two compose, but neither requires the other.

input.old_string:
[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is a set of bash scripts enabling enterprise grade container image management intended for incorporation into any project.
The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI.
After initial manual setup, all cloud API calls use `openssl` + `curl`.
A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise.
[Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards.

## Environment

[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable [provenance](#Provenance), and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation.
The two compose but neither requires the other.

### Supported Platforms

input.new_string: ## Appendix: How This Is Normally Done The two controls [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides are not novel — they are what a platform team normally assembles from dedicated infrastructure. This appendix names that conventional stack, so the comparison stays honest and the trade-off stays legible. ### Controlled container builds Knowing where an image came from — and proving it — is the domain of software supply-chain security. The stabilized toolchain pairs build provenance ([SLSA](https://slsa.dev)) with cryptographic signing ([Sigstore](https://www.sigstore.dev)/cosign), a [software bill of materials](#SBOM) ([Syft](https://github.com/anchore/syft)), vulnerability scanning, and deploy-time admission control ([Kyverno](https://kyverno.io), OPA Gatekeeper) that rejects unsigned or unattested images. Reaching SLSA Build Level 2 with this stack is a matter of weeks; the [Foundry](#Foundry) reaches **Level 3** with none of it resident on the workstation. ### Restricting access Constraining what a workload can reach on the internet is the domain of network egress control. At the corporate-network tier this is a [secure web gateway](https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cyberpedia/what-is-secure-web-gateway), increasingly bundled into [SASE](https://www.checkpoint.com/cyber-hub/network-security/what-is-secure-access-service-edge-sase/) alongside a CASB and firewall. At the container tier it is [Kubernetes NetworkPolicy](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/network-policies/), usually upgraded to [Cilium](https://cilium.io/use-cases/egress-gateway/) or Calico for DNS-aware, deny-by-default egress. That "deny-by-default, allow only where needed" posture is exactly the [Sentry](#Sentry) model. **Honest scope.** These are multi-tenant corporate systems, centrally administered and kept alive by a standing team. [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) puts the same two controls in reach of a small team or a solo developer; it is not a fleet-scale replacement for a secure web gateway or a service mesh, and does not try to be. The value is the control, at a cost a small team can carry. ## Appendix: Foundry Operations

The stabilized toolchain pairs build provenance ([SLSA](https://slsa.dev)) with cryptographic signing ([Sigstore](https://www.sigstore.dev)/cosign), a [software bill of materials](#SBOM) ([Syft](https://github.com/anchore/syft)), vulnerability scanning, and deploy-time admission control ([Kyverno](https://kyverno.io), OPA Gatekeeper) that rejects unsigned or unattested images. Reaching SLSA Build Level 2 with this stack is a matter of weeks; the [Foundry](#Foundry) reaches **Level 3** with none of it resident on the workstation. ### Restricting access Constraining what a workload can reach on the internet is the domain of network egress control. At the corporate-network tier this is a [secure web gateway](https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cyberpedia/what-is-secure-web-gateway), increasingly bundled into [SASE](https://www.checkpoint.com/cyber-hub/network-security/what-is-secure-access-service-edge-sase/) alongside a CASB and firewall. At the container tier it is [Kubernetes NetworkPolicy](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/network-policies/), usually upgraded to [Cilium](https://cilium.io/use-cases/egress-gateway/) or Calico for DNS-aware, deny-by-default egress. That "deny-by-default, allow only where needed" posture is exactly the [Sentry](#Sentry) model. **Honest scope.** These are multi-tenant corporate systems, centrally administered and kept alive by a standing team. [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) puts the same two controls in reach of a small team or a solo developer; it is not a fleet-scale replacement for a secure web gateway or a service mesh, and does not try to be. The value is the control, at a cost a small team can carry. ## Appendix: Foundry Operations

lines: The project maintainer release qualification ceremony — five operator steps, roughly one hour wall-clock, with cloud cost on the order of two GCP projects per run.
See [RELEASE.md](RELEASE.md) for the full procedure.
+## Appendix: How This Is Normally Done

+The two controls [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides are not novel — they are what a platform team normally assembles from dedicated infrastructure. This appendix names that conventional stack, so the comparison stays honest and the trade-off stays legible.
+### Controlled container builds

+Knowing where an image came from — and proving it — is the domain of software supply-chain security. The stabilized toolchain pairs build provenance ([SLSA](https://slsa.dev)) with cryptographic signing ([Sigstore](https://www.sigstore.dev)/cosign), a [software bill of materials](#SBOM) ([Syft](https://github.com/anchore/syft)), vulnerability scanning, and deploy-time admission control ([Kyverno](https://kyverno.io), OPA Gatekeeper) that rejects unsigned or unattested images.
+Reaching SLSA Build Level 2 with this stack is a matter of weeks; the [Foundry](#Foundry) reaches **Level 3** with none of it resident on the workstation.
+### Restricting access

+Constraining what a workload can reach on the internet is the domain of network egress control. At the corporate-network tier this is a [secure web gateway](https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cyberpedia/what-is-secure-web-gateway), increasingly bundled into [SASE](https://www.checkpoint.com/cyber-hub/network-security/what-is-secure-access-service-edge-sase/) alongside a CASB and firewall. At the container tier it is [Kubernetes NetworkPolicy](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/network-policies/), usually upgraded to [Cilium](https://cilium.io/use-cases/egress-gateway/) or Calico for DNS-aware, deny-by-default egress.
+That "deny-by-default, allow only where needed" posture is exactly the [Sentry](#Sentry) model.

+**Honest scope.** These are multi-tenant corporate systems, centrally administered and kept alive by a standing team. [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) puts the same two controls in reach of a small team or a solo developer; it is not a fleet-scale replacement for a secure web gateway or a service mesh, and does not try to be. The value is the control, at a cost a small team can carry.
## Appendix: Foundry Operations
Formal definitions for all [Foundry](#Foundry) operations, organized by lifecycle phase.

content.content:
1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 Containers give you control over what runs. Getting that control to hold at the *edges* — [knowing where an image actually came from](#ControlledContainerBuilds), and [constraining what it can reach once it runs](#RestrictingAccess) — is normally a standing job for a platform team. 4 5 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) collapses that cost. It's a set of bash scripts — `bash 3.2`, `curl`, `openssl`, `jq`, and a handful of standard tools, with no Python runtime, no language package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI — that a small team can use to stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without one. After initial setup, every cloud API call is `openssl` + `curl`. 6 7 It extends a container's control to its two edges: 8 9 - **[Foundry](#Foundry)** — *where images come from.* Orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform images (x86 + ARM) with verifiable [SLSA provenance](#Provenance), serves them from a role-managed private cloud registry, and can build [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) so a compromised build step cannot phone home. 10 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)** — *what images can reach.* Runs untrusted containers — even images pulled unmodified "in the wild" — behind enforced network isolation: DNS and IP filtering that a compromised workload cannot bypass. 11 12 The two compose, but neither requires the other. 13 14 > [!IMPORTANT] 15 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** 16 > 17 > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation chain, [build isolation](#BuildIsolation), and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 18 > 19 > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. 20 > 21 > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. 22 > Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 23 24 **Host platform scope.** [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is release-1 qualified on Linux and macOS with Docker. Windows host support works and is exercised in testing, but is not yet part of the release-1 qualification baseline — treat it as supported-experimental for now. 25 26 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 27 28

29 30

31 32 ## Environment 33 34 ### Supported Platforms 35 36 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime is qualified for release-1 against **Docker** as the container runtime, on two host families: 37 38 - **Linux host** — native Docker Engine (Docker CE / `docker.io`) running directly on the host kernel; no VM. Tested on Ubuntu LTS with cgroup v2 and a 6.x kernel. 39 - **macOS host** — Docker Desktop for Mac on a supported macOS release. Apple Silicon hosts use the Apple Virtualization framework or Docker VMM hypervisor backend. 40 41 Windows host support works — [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) runs on Windows via Docker Desktop — but is not yet part of the release-1 qualification baseline; treat it as supported-experimental pending a green Windows test pass. 42 Podman support is architecturally accommodated by the spec but deferred — see [Podman Support](#PodmanSupport). 43 44 All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. 45 Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRD](#RBRD)), repository-wide settings ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). 46 Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)).

file.content:
# Recipe Bottle Containers give you control over what runs. Getting that control to hold at the *edges* — [knowing where an image actually came from](#ControlledContainerBuilds), and [constraining what it can reach once it runs](#RestrictingAccess) — is normally a standing job for a platform team. [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) collapses that cost. It's a set of bash scripts — `bash 3.2`, `curl`, `openssl`, `jq`, and a handful of standard tools, with no Python runtime, no language package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI — that a small team can use to stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without one. After initial setup, every cloud API call is `openssl` + `curl`. It extends a container's control to its two edges: - **[Foundry](#Foundry)** — *where images come from.* Orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform images (x86 + ARM) with verifiable [SLSA provenance](#Provenance), serves them from a role-managed private cloud registry, and can build [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) so a compromised build step cannot phone home. - **[Crucible](#Crucible)** — *what images can reach.* Runs untrusted containers — even images pulled unmodified "in the wild" — behind enforced network isolation: DNS and IP filtering that a compromised workload cannot bypass. The two compose, but neither requires the other. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** > > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation chain, [build isolation](#BuildIsolation), and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. > > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. > > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. > Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. **Host platform scope.** [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is release-1 qualified on Linux and macOS with Docker. Windows host support works and is exercised in testing, but is not yet part of the release-1 qualification baseline — treat it as supported-experimental for now. **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle

## Environment ### Supported Platforms [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime is qualified for release-1 against **Docker** as the container runtime, on two host families: - **Linux host** — native Docker Engine (Docker CE / `docker.io`) running directly on the host kernel; no VM. Tested on Ubuntu LTS with cgroup v2 and a 6.x kernel. - **macOS host** — Docker Desktop for Mac on a supported macOS release. Apple Silicon hosts use the Apple Virtualization framework or Docker VMM hypervisor backend. Windows host support works — [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) runs on Windows via Docker Desktop — but is not yet part of the release-1 qualification baseline; treat it as supported-experimental pending a green Windows test pass. Podman support is architecturally accommodated by the spec but deferred — see [Podman Support](#PodmanSupport). All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRD](#RBRD)), repository-wide settings ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)).

content.content: 250 251 ## Appendix: How This Is Normally Done 252 253 The two controls [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides are not novel — they are what a platform team normally assembles from dedicated infrastructure. This appendix names that conventional stack, so the comparison stays honest and the trade-off stays legible. 254 255 ### Controlled container builds 256 257 Knowing where an image came from — and proving it — is the domain of software supply-chain security. The stabilized toolchain pairs build provenance ([SLSA](https://slsa.dev)) with cryptographic signing ([Sigstore](https://www.sigstore.dev)/cosign), a [software bill of materials](#SBOM) ([Syft](https://github.com/anchore/syft)), vulnerability scanning, and deploy-time admission control ([Kyverno](https://kyverno.io), OPA Gatekeeper) that rejects unsigned or unattested images. 258 Reaching SLSA Build Level 2 with this stack is a matter of weeks; the [Foundry](#Foundry) reaches **Level 3** with none of it resident on the workstation. 259 260 ### Restricting access 261 262 Constraining what a workload can reach on the internet is the domain of network egress control. At the corporate-network tier this is a [secure web gateway](https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cyberpedia/what-is-secure-web-gateway), increasingly bundled into [SASE](https://www.checkpoint.com/cyber-hub/network-security/what-is-secure-access-service-edge-sase/) alongside a CASB and firewall. At the container tier it is [Kubernetes NetworkPolicy](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/network-policies/), usually upgraded to [Cilium](https://cilium.io/use-cases/egress-gateway/) or Calico for DNS-aware, deny-by-default egress. 263 That "deny-by-default, allow only where needed" posture is exactly the [Sentry](#Sentry) model. 264 265 **Honest scope.** These are multi-tenant corporate systems, centrally administered and kept alive by a standing team. [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) puts the same two controls in reach of a small team or a solo developer; it is not a fleet-scale replacement for a secure web gateway or a service mesh, and does not try to be. The value is the control, at a cost a small team can carry. 266 267 ## Appendix: Foundry Operations 268 269 Formal definitions for all [Foundry](#Foundry) operations, organized by lifecycle phase. 270 271 ### Infrastructure 272 273 **[Establish](#Establish)** — Guided setup of a new [Manor](#Manor) — creates the [Manor's](#Manor) GCP project and configures the OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. 274 [Establishing](#Establish) walks the [Payor](#Payor) through project creation, API enablement, and consent screen configuration — the manual prerequisites before any automated operations can run. 275 276 **[Install](#Install)** — Ingest OAuth client credentials from a downloaded JSON key file. 277 [Installing](#Install) triggers a browser authorization flow and stores the resulting refresh token locally with restrictive permissions (`600`). 278 This is a one-time [Payor](#Payor) operation — the refresh token persists until explicitly revoked. 279

What landed in the README:

Worth a look on GitHub once it renders, to confirm the two opener links land on the appendix sub-anchors the way you pictured.

message.content: We are definitely on the right track.  I wonder that we ought to emphasize in second paragraph that this is a deeply restricted set of dependencies for ability to run everywhere with a rigorously shrunken exposure interface.  Thoughts?

content.text:
We're definitely on the right track, and yes — I think your instinct is strong, but it's actually *two* virtues hiding in one fact, and they want slightly different handling.

The narrow dependency set buys three things, and right now paragraph 2 only voices the first:
1. **Low adoption cost** (no DevOps team) — already there.
2. **Shrunken exposure** — fewer dependencies = a smaller attack surface, fewer CVEs to chase, almost nothing of its own to audit, patch, or trust.
3. **Portability** — nothing language-specific to install; it runs wherever the standard Unix tools already are.

**On #2 — strong yes, and it's better than "nice to have."** It's *thematically self-consistent*: a tool whose whole job is hardening your supply chain shouldn't drag in a sprawling supply chain of its own. That argument elevates the pitch from "cheap" to "principled," and it's true — `bash`/`curl`/`openssl`/`jq` is a trusted base small enough to actually read. I'd lead the new emphasis here.

**On #3 — yes, but with a precise hand, or we create a fresh contradiction.** "Runs everywhere" is true of the *control plane* (the bash + cloud-API layer runs anywhere those tools exist). It is **not** true of the whole system: the [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime needs Docker and Linux container primitives, and the platform-scope note just below says release-1 is Linux/macOS-with-Docker, Windows experimental. So if paragraph 2 claims the *system* "runs everywhere," it collides with line 24 eight lines later. The honest, still-compelling version is "nothing language-specific to install / depends only on what's already on the workstation" — portability of the *footprint*, not a universal-runtime claim.

Here's a candidate paragraph 2 to react to:

> [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) collapses that cost. It's a set of bash scripts with a deliberately tiny dependency surface — `bash 3.2`, `curl`, `openssl`, `jq`, and a handful of standard tools, with no Python runtime, no language package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI — so there is nothing language-specific to install and almost nothing of its own to audit, patch, or trust. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without one; after initial setup, every cloud API call is `openssl` + `curl`.

Two dials:
- **The self-consistency kicker.** I kept it implicit ("almost nothing of its own to … trust"). If you want it explicit, I'd add one line: *"a tool that hardens your supply chain shouldn't expand it."* Strong, slightly aphoristic — your call whether it earns the space or tips into flourish.
- **How hard to push portability.** I scoped it to "nothing to install" to stay honest against the Docker requirement. If you want "runs everywhere" louder, we'd need to say *which* layer — and I'd want to keep the runtime caveat visible so the platform-scope note doesn't read as a walk-back.

Want me to land this version, add the kicker, or push the portability harder first?

message.content: That looks better.  Help me decide if we mention that the regression test suite is written in rust and thus has a few more dependencies, but optional for our users to do that.

content.text:
Good catch — and I think raising it is exactly right, because it protects the claim we just strengthened. A security-minded reader *will* clone the repo, see `Cargo.toml` and a pile of `.rs` files, and if the README said "tiny dependency surface, no language package manager" with no qualification, we'd look like we oversold. Pre-empting that is sincerity, not hedging.

Here's how I'd decide it:

**Mention it — yes. But not in the hook, and frame it as a feature, not an apology.**

The honest split is *use* vs *test/contribute*:
- **To run Recipe Bottle:** the lean toolchain only. No host Rust — even building the Rust Ifrit bottle happens *inside* Docker, not on your workstation.
- **To run the regression + adversarial suites (or contribute):** a Rust toolchain, because Theurge is Rust.

And here's why that's a feature: the IMPORTANT box right above already invites people to *verify our containment claims themselves*. The Rust suite **is** how they do that. So the note isn't "sorry, there's more stuff" — it's "the toolchain to *use* it is tiny; the toolchain to *adversarially validate* it is a superset you only need if you're checking our work." That lands perfectly with the audience the box is courting.

**Two placement principles:**
1. Keep paragraph 2 crisp — a test-dependency caveat doesn't belong in the hook.
2. Put it where "what do I need installed?" questions get answered — end of the **Supported Platforms** block is the natural home.

Candidate sentence for there:

> Those are the only dependencies for *running* Recipe Bottle. The regression and adversarial test suites are written in Rust, so validating containment yourself — or contributing — additionally needs a Rust toolchain; using Recipe Bottle does not.

**One integrity sub-point regardless of the note:** the phrase "no language package manager" in paragraph 2 is technically falsified by the in-repo `cargo` *unless* the claim is scoped to the user. Two ways to keep it bulletproof: (a) lean on the new note to draw the line, or (b) tweak the lead-in to "*Running it* needs only…" so the list is plainly about what RB asks of *you*, not the repo's total composition. I'd do (b) as cheap insurance even with the note — your call.

So the decision I'd recommend: **add the Rust note at the end of Supported Platforms, framed as "how you validate it yourself," plus the small (b) scoping tweak to paragraph 2.** Want me to land both, just the note, or adjust the framing first?

1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 Containers give you control over what runs. Getting that control to hold at the *edges* — [knowing where an image actually came from](#ControlledContainerBuilds), and [constraining what it can reach once it runs](#RestrictingAccess) — is normally a standing job for a platform team. 4 5 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) collapses that cost. It's a set of bash scripts — `bash 3.2`, `curl`, `openssl`, `jq`, and a handful of standard tools, with no Python runtime, no language package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI — that a small team can use to stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without one. After initial setup, every cloud API call is `openssl` + `curl`. 6 7 It extends a container's control to its two edges: 8 9 - **[Foundry](#Foundry)** — *where images come from.* Orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform images (x86 + ARM) with verifiable [SLSA provenance](#Provenance), serves them from a role-managed private cloud registry, and can build [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) so a compromised build step cannot phone home. 10 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)** — *what images can reach.* Runs untrusted containers — even images pulled unmodified "in the wild" — behind enforced network isolation: DNS and IP filtering that a compromised workload cannot bypass. 11 12 The two compose, but neither requires the other. 13 14 > [!IMPORTANT] 15 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** 16 > 17 > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation chain, [build isolation](#BuildIsolation), and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 18 > 19 > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. 20 > 21 > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. 22 > Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 23 24 **Host platform scope.** [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is release-1 qualified on Linux and macOS with Docker. Windows host support works and is exercised in testing, but is not yet part of the release-1 qualification baseline — treat it as supported-experimental for now. 25 26 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 27 28

29 30

31 32 ## Environment 33 34 ### Supported Platforms 35 36 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime is qualified for release-1 against **Docker** as the container runtime, on two host families: 37 38 - **Linux host** — native Docker Engine (Docker CE / `docker.io`) running directly on the host kernel; no VM. Tested on Ubuntu LTS with cgroup v2 and a 6.x kernel. 39 - **macOS host** — Docker Desktop for Mac on a supported macOS release. Apple Silicon hosts use the Apple Virtualization framework or Docker VMM hypervisor backend. 40 41 Windows host support works — [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) runs on Windows via Docker Desktop — but is not yet part of the release-1 qualification baseline; treat it as supported-experimental pending a green Windows test pass. 42 Podman support is architecturally accommodated by the spec but deferred — see [Podman Support](#PodmanSupport). 43 44 All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. 45 Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRD](#RBRD)), repository-wide settings ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). 46 Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). 47 48 Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. 49 The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. 50 Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands.

file.content:
# Recipe Bottle Containers give you control over what runs. Getting that control to hold at the *edges* — [knowing where an image actually came from](#ControlledContainerBuilds), and [constraining what it can reach once it runs](#RestrictingAccess) — is normally a standing job for a platform team. [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) collapses that cost. It's a set of bash scripts — `bash 3.2`, `curl`, `openssl`, `jq`, and a handful of standard tools, with no Python runtime, no language package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI — that a small team can use to stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without one. After initial setup, every cloud API call is `openssl` + `curl`. It extends a container's control to its two edges: - **[Foundry](#Foundry)** — *where images come from.* Orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform images (x86 + ARM) with verifiable [SLSA provenance](#Provenance), serves them from a role-managed private cloud registry, and can build [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) so a compromised build step cannot phone home. - **[Crucible](#Crucible)** — *what images can reach.* Runs untrusted containers — even images pulled unmodified "in the wild" — behind enforced network isolation: DNS and IP filtering that a compromised workload cannot bypass. The two compose, but neither requires the other. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** > > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation chain, [build isolation](#BuildIsolation), and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. > > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. > > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. > Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. **Host platform scope.** [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is release-1 qualified on Linux and macOS with Docker. Windows host support works and is exercised in testing, but is not yet part of the release-1 qualification baseline — treat it as supported-experimental for now. **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle

## Environment ### Supported Platforms [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime is qualified for release-1 against **Docker** as the container runtime, on two host families: - **Linux host** — native Docker Engine (Docker CE / `docker.io`) running directly on the host kernel; no VM. Tested on Ubuntu LTS with cgroup v2 and a 6.x kernel. - **macOS host** — Docker Desktop for Mac on a supported macOS release. Apple Silicon hosts use the Apple Virtualization framework or Docker VMM hypervisor backend. Windows host support works — [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) runs on Windows via Docker Desktop — but is not yet part of the release-1 qualification baseline; treat it as supported-experimental pending a green Windows test pass. Podman support is architecturally accommodated by the spec but deferred — see [Podman Support](#PodmanSupport). All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRD](#RBRD)), repository-wide settings ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands.

"running it needs only" scopes the claim to *use*, so the in-repo Rust can't falsify it; "nothing language-specific to install" is the honest portability note; "almost nothing of its own to audit, patch, or trust" is the shrunken-exposure point.

content.text:
Checked, and here's the honest picture.

**"Qualified" is well-defined** — by `RELEASE.md`. It means a platform/runtime has been validated by passing the **release-qualification ceremony**: the maintainer-run, *from-pristine* procedure (marshal-zero the repo to a blank template, then `rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh` runs the entire lifecycle from clean state — five steps, ~1 hour, ~two throwaway GCP projects). Its whole reason for existing is to catch "silent first-build assumptions" that the everyday `QualifyFast`/`QualifyRelease` tiers tolerate. So *"release-1 qualified against Docker on Linux and macOS"* means: **the maintainer has run that clean-state ceremony on those host/runtime combinations and it passes** — Windows hasn't been through it yet, hence "supported-experimental."

**"release-1" is *not* defined anywhere.** It appears three times — all in the README (lines 24, 36, 41) — and nowhere else. `RELEASE.md`, which is *the* release document, never uses the string "release-1" at all; it just says "the release qualification ceremony." So the `-1` milestone label has no referent a reader can chase. I'm *inferring* it means "the first public release," but that's me filling a gap, not reading a definition.

So bluntly: a cold reader meeting "release-1 qualified" in the very first platform line (225 lines before the Release Procedure section explains the ceremony) gets insider shorthand with no anchor. That's the same sterility/opacity we've been hunting — clear to you, opaque to them.

Two honest ways to fix it, if you want:
1. **Define it once** — gloss the first use, or link "release-1 qualified" at line 24 to the [Release Procedure](#Release-Procedure)/`RELEASE.md` so "qualified" gains its meaning on first contact.
2. **Plainer language** — drop the milestone label and say what it *is*: "validated end-to-end from a clean state" / "passes the full from-scratch release ceremony."

My lean: keep "qualified" (it's a real, earned word backed by the ceremony) but either link it on first use or add a four-word gloss, and reconsider whether "release-1" earns its place at all given nothing defines the "1." Want me to pick one and land it, or talk it through first?

lastPrompt: What does `release-1 qualified` mean?

Implements the README half of the BH-heat-related documentation work — preparing the public-facing claims for the revised-enshrine rework signaled in RBSHR by the prior two commits.
7124cec70 2026-05-06  jjb:1011-66aa5e5d:₣A-:n: Add §Supported Platforms qualifying release-1 against native Docker on Linux, Docker Desktop on Windows (WSL2 backend with per-distro integration, single-daemon), and Docker Desktop on Mac (Apple Virtualization or Docker VMM). Aligns with RBS0 §at_runtime stance (Docker today, Podman deferred); points readers at §Future Work for the Podman accommodation. Linux qualification verified by inspecting cerebro (Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS, kernel 6.8.0-107, Docker 28.1.1, cgroup v2/systemd) currently running rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh — the release-gate suite consumed by ₣BB rbk-mvp-3-release-qualification. Inserted as H3 inside §Environment after the two-capability summary so the qualification appears before any setup-flow mentions; complements paddock ₣A- §Standing Notes Docker Desktop entry landed in commit 66aa5e5d.

content.text:
The term traces back cleanly. Three commits touched it:

- **`7124cec70` — 2026-05-06 (heat `₣A-`) — this is the introduction.** It added the `§Supported Platforms` section, "qualifying release-1 against native Docker on Linux, Docker Desktop on Windows (WSL2 backend…), and Docker Desktop on Mac." The commit message ties it to heat `₣BB rbk-mvp-3-release-qualification` and the `gauntlet` release-gate suite — so "release-1" was minted as the **first-release milestone** that the MVP-3 qualification work + the `RELEASE.md` ceremony validate against.
- **`eef0cc49f` — 2026-05-13 (heat `₣BH`)** — corrected it: the original commit had claimed **Windows as release-1 qualified, but it had never been tested** — "the claim was inaccurate." Shrank to Linux + macOS and added the top-of-README host-platform-scope note.
- **`4d4d94cf5` — 2026-06-02 (heat `₣BV`)** — your prior pass: flipped Windows to "supported-experimental," roadmap/anchoring work.

Two things worth saying plainly:

1. **It was a milestone label from birth and never carried a definition.** The introducing commit used "qualifying release-1" as shorthand for "the first-release qualification milestone," and no commit since has glossed the `-1` in the doc. So my earlier read holds: clear to you and the heat structure, opaque to a cold reader.

2. **This exact phrase has already caused one accuracy slip** — the May-6 introduction over-claimed Windows, corrected a week later. That's a real, not theoretical, argument for tightening it: a milestone-label-without-a-definition invites both reader confusion *and* author over-claim, and the history shows the latter already happened once.

So the "release-1" you're asking about is genuinely a homegrown term with no anchor, born ~4 weeks ago alongside the Supported Platforms section. That strengthens the case for the fix from last turn — define it on first use or replace it with what it actually means ("passes the full from-pristine release ceremony"). Want me to pick one and land it, or keep it as-is now that you know the lineage?

file.content:
[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) uses a role-based security model with four roles, each building on the previous:

| Role | Authenticates via | Purpose |
|------|-------------------|---------|
| [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [Governor](#Governor) lifecycle |
| [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [Depot](#Depot) |
| [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies [provenance](#Provenance) |
| [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use |

The [Payor](#Payor) stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication.
All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation.
Every role's credential is an on-disk key file and **no GCP organization is required**; admitting operators through an external identity provider instead is the planned [Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation) tier.

#### Establishment and Provisioning

The [Payor](#Payor) begins by [Establishing](#Establish) a GCP project and configuring an OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console.
After downloading the OAuth client credentials, the [Payor](#Payor) [Installs](#Install) them via a browser authorization flow — the resulting refresh token is stored locally with restrictive permissions and does not need to be repeated.

With [Payor](#Payor) credentials in place, the [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), provisioning it with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets storage.
The [Payor](#Payor) then [Mantles](#Mantle) a [Governor](#Governor) service account to administer the [Depot](#Depot).

Before the first build can run, the [Depot](#Depot) needs its supply-chain infrastructure in place: upstream base images must be [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the registry, and a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) of builder tool images must be inscribed.

#### Credential Distribution

The [Governor](#Governor) [Invests](#Invest) downstream credentials: a [Director](#Director) for build operations and a [Retriever](#Retriever) for image pull access.
Each credential is scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot).

content.content: 505 506 - **[Bottle Credential Custody](#BottleCredentialCustody)** - Move the service secrets a [Bottle](#Bottle) workload uses (cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) off the operator's workstation and into the [Bottle](#Bottle) itself, injected at [Charge](#Charge) time via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration — never baked into the image. 507 The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds the secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge, which blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation. 508 Orthogonal to but paired with the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit): the conduit gives the [Bottle](#Bottle) network reach to a cloud service while the [Sentry](#Sentry) holds the tunnel key, and this holds the service secret inside the [Bottle](#Bottle). 509 510 - **[Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation)** - Admit operators through an external identity provider (OIDC/SAML) instead of distributing service-account key files — identity proved by a fresh sign-in, capabilities granted to a federated principal, no secret on disk. 511 This tier **requires a GCP organization**, which is the dividing line from today's keyfile model (which needs none): a qualifying organization is free via Google Cloud Identity once you verify ownership of a **DNS domain**, so the real prerequisite is controlling a domain, not paying Google. 512 Revocation becomes central at the identity provider rather than per-keyfile rotation. 513 514 - **[VPC Service Controls](#VpcServiceControls)** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. 515 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 516 If a VPC is stood up for the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit) architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers.

file.content: - **[Bottle Credential Custody](#BottleCredentialCustody)** - Move the service secrets a [Bottle](#Bottle) workload uses (cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) off the operator's workstation and into the [Bottle](#Bottle) itself, injected at [Charge](#Charge) time via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration — never baked into the image. The workstation [Charges](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds the secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge, which blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation. Orthogonal to but paired with the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit): the conduit gives the [Bottle](#Bottle) network reach to a cloud service while the [Sentry](#Sentry) holds the tunnel key, and this holds the service secret inside the [Bottle](#Bottle). - **[Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation)** - Admit operators through an external identity provider (OIDC/SAML) instead of distributing service-account key files — identity proved by a fresh sign-in, capabilities granted to a federated principal, no secret on disk. This tier **requires a GCP organization**, which is the dividing line from today's keyfile model (which needs none): a qualifying organization is free via Google Cloud Identity once you verify ownership of a **DNS domain**, so the real prerequisite is controlling a domain, not paying Google. Revocation becomes central at the identity provider rather than per-keyfile rotation. - **[VPC Service Controls](#VpcServiceControls)** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. If a VPC is stood up for the [Crucible Conduit](#CrucibleConduit) architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers.

aiTitle: Refine README.md with roadmap alignment review

content.text:
Assessed cold. Here's my honest read — and the short version is: the README is clear about the **fact** ("no org required") but oblique about the **consequence** ("what running org-less actually means").

**Where it's clear (the barrier):** Line 152, at the end of the role table, is well-placed and unambiguous — *"Every role's credential is an on-disk key file and **no GCP organization is required**."* A reader learns up front that the zero-barrier path exists. Good.

**Where it's not (the consequences):** A reader who absorbs "no org required" is left with three unanswered questions, and the answers are scattered or missing:

1. **Is no-org a *complete* model or a *crippled* one?** The README never says plainly that you lose **no capability** by going org-less — everything works; an org would only buy you keyless auth and central revocation. Without that reassurance, a careful reader can't tell whether "no org required" means "fully supported" or "degraded-but-tolerated."

2. **What's the security tradeoff I'm accepting?** The real consequence of no-org is: **secrets live on disk, and your revocation story is per-keyfile rotation** (Divest / re-key), not central. That's stated *only* as a contrast clause inside the **Operator Federation roadmap entry** (line 512, *"per-keyfile rotation"*) — 360 lines away from where the operator meets the credential model, and framed as a property of a *future* tier rather than a description of the *present* one. The present consequence has no present home.

3. **For the audience you're courting specifically.** The IMPORTANT box invites security reviewers, and "where do credentials rest, and how are they revoked?" is *exactly* the no-org consequence. Right now that reviewer has to reverse-engineer it from a roadmap bullet.

**My read:** this is a real but modest gap — the facts are all in the document, but the *present-tense consequence of the default tier* is implied rather than stated, and it's the thing your target reader most wants spelled out. Not a glaring error; a clarity sharpening.

**If you want it fixed:** the clean move is one or two sentences near line 152 (where the keyfile model is introduced) that state the consequence directly — *no-org is fully functional; the tradeoff is on-disk credentials whose revocation is manual per-keyfile (Divest/rotate); an org would add keyless auth and central revocation* — with the [Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation) link carrying the future-tier detail as it does now. That gives the present consequence a present home without bloating the roadmap entry.

One check before I'd draft: **which consequence are you most concerned a reader misreads** — that no-org sounds *crippled* (a capability worry), or that no-org sounds *free of tradeoffs* (a security-posture worry)? They pull the sentence in slightly different directions, and I'd rather aim at the one you're seeing.

message.content: In short, I think no-org is enough to assess Recipe Bottle with but unlikely to be acceptable in the long run- having permanent access tokens outside a full IdP is going to be too much for real corporate use.

content.text:
That's the sharper, truer framing — and I think you've put your finger on exactly what the README is currently too neutral about. Let me confirm it, sharpen one technical point, and flag a tone risk before we touch anything.

**Your instinct is correct, and it's the industry posture, not just a preference.** Long-lived, downloadable service-account keys are precisely the thing corporate security programs (and Google's own guidance) push *away* from, in favor of short-lived, federated, centrally-revocable identity. So "fine to assess / unacceptable for real corporate use long-term" isn't self-criticism — it's an accurate read of where the bar sits. Stating it *builds* credibility with the security audience the IMPORTANT box courts: it shows you understand corporate posture rather than overselling.

**One precision worth getting right in the wording:** the permanent thing on disk is the service-account **key** (the RBRA file), not the access token. The tokens it mints are short-lived (`RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC`); the *key that can keep minting them until someone rotates it* is the standing liability. So the honest phrase is "long-lived key on disk," not "permanent access tokens" — and that's the exact thing corporate review rejects.

**The tone risk to steer around:** there's a real difference between *"keyfile is the evaluation on-ramp; corporate production wants federation (planned)"* (confident scoping — good) and *"this isn't production-ready"* (self-sabotage — bad). And we must not accidentally walk back the whole README's pitch: a **small team** genuinely *can* run this for real on keyfiles. The line you're drawing is **corporate/compliance-bound** adoption, not all production. So the framing is: *enough to evaluate, and fine at small-team scale; what it won't clear long-term is a corporate bar that rejects static on-disk keys.*

Here's a candidate to replace line 152 (the present consequence's natural home):

> Every role's credential is an on-disk service-account key, and **no GCP organization is required** — which makes this tier ideal for evaluating Recipe Bottle and for running it at small-team scale. The tradeoff is honest: a long-lived key at rest on disk, revoked by rotation rather than centrally. That posture is unlikely to clear a corporate security bar in the long run — which is why admitting operators through a full external identity provider (short-lived sign-in, central revocation, no static key on disk) is the planned [Operator Federation](#OperatorFederation) tier.

That gives the present consequence a present home, keeps the small-team pitch intact, and hands the org-required / free-via-domain detail to the roadmap entry as it stands.

Two things for you: does that land the "assess now, corporate-later" line where you want it, and do you want me to also nudge the **Operator Federation roadmap bullet** to lead with "the path to corporate-acceptable identity" rather than just "an alternative auth mechanism"? Say the word and I'll land it.

aiTitle: Refine README.md with roadmap alignment review

content.text:
You're right, and I was wrong. I read the actual lifecycle and the spec backs your version, not my wording.

**The evidence** (`RBSGS-GettingStarted.adoc`, Recovery + credential sections):
- **Revoking** a Director/Retriever = **[Divest](#Divest)** — the Governor *deletes the service account*, invalidating the credential. A manual operation.
- **Governor** compromise/rotation = **Mantle** — the Payor *replaces* the SA with a fresh one. Also manual.
- Nothing sets an expiry on the SA **key**. What expires is the short-lived OAuth *token* minted from it (and the Payor's OAuth refresh token), not the key on disk. The key is permanent until a human kills it. `RBSHR` even calls this "the keyfile tier's … rotation burden."

So my phrase **"revoked by rotation"** was wrong on two counts: revocation is by *deletion/divest*, not rotation; and there's no automatic rotation at all — it's all manual, per-key, and the key is permanent until then. Your read — "permanent, must be manually revised" — is the accurate one.

Correcting both the clause I wrote *and* a pre-existing twin of the same error in the roadmap bullet (it also said "per-keyfile rotation"):

input.old_string: The tradeoff is honest: a long-lived key at rest on disk, revoked by rotation rather than centrally.
input.new_string: The tradeoff is honest: that key is long-lived, does not expire on its own, and stays valid until a human revokes it by hand (deleting the account) — there is no central or automatic revocation.

Per RBSGS Recovery section, the on-disk service-account key is permanent (no auto-expiry; only the minted OAuth token is short-lived), and revocation is a manual operation — Divest deletes a Director/Retriever SA, Mantle replaces the Governor SA — not 'rotation.' Replace the Foundry-Lifecycle clause 'revoked by rotation rather than centrally' with the accurate statement that the key is long-lived, does not expire on its own, and stays valid until manually revoked by deleting the account (no central or automatic revocation). Also fix the twin error in the Operator Federation roadmap bullet ('per-keyfile rotation' -> 'a manual, per-key Divest', linked)."}

content.text: 2f3dbaa82266d729f73f0cf59a635afd0b87d54b

Committed `2f3dbaa82` (`₣BV`, README only), ready for your push.

The corrected text now reads:

- **Line 152:** *"…that key is long-lived, does not expire on its own, and stays valid until a human revokes it by hand (deleting the account) — there is no central or automatic revocation."*
- **Operator Federation bullet:** *"Revocation becomes central at the identity provider rather than a manual, per-key [Divest](#Divest)."*

Thanks for catching it — that was a real error, not a quibble. "Revoked by rotation" would have told a security reviewer something false about the exact property they care most about, and it would've sat there under my name. The permanence of the on-disk key *is* the corporate-unacceptable fact, so getting it precise actually sharpens the "assess-now, corporate-later" argument rather than weakening it.

Still open whenever you want them: the **Podman framing contradiction** (your truth-call), **"136 Tabtargets" → ~199**, and **Reference Nameplates omitting srjcl/pluml**.

content.content: README.md:5:[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) collapses that cost. It's a set of bash scripts with a deliberately tiny dependency surface: running it needs only `bash 3.2`, `curl`, `openssl`, `jq`, and a handful of standard tools — no Python runtime, no language package manager, no `gcloud` CLI — so there is nothing language-specific to install and almost nothing of its own to audit, patch, or trust. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without one; after initial setup, every cloud API call is `openssl` + `curl`. README.md:19:> The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. content.text:
Checked every Podman/Lode/runtime mention. Here's the precise answer.

**For the README: yes — and it's *already* in the state you want, so there's essentially nothing to land.**
- Podman appears in exactly two substantive places, and **both already name it as future/deferred**:
  - Line 42: *"Podman support is architecturally accommodated by the spec but deferred — see Podman Support."*
  - Roadmap `#PodmanSupport` (530–532): *"The spec accommodates Podman… but support is deferred… no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop."*
- **Lode / podvm appears nowhere in the README.** So "no mention of saving the lode for podman vm" is already true — nothing to remove, and nothing about the upcoming Lode-podvm work would leak in. The other "runtime" hits are all benign (Crucible/Sentry/Pentacle runtime, RBRR/RBRN config).

So your directive is effectively already implemented. I'd not manufacture an edit just to look busy.

**Two honest caveats, though:**

1. **One phrase hints at present-tense design accommodation** — "the spec accommodates Podman" (both spots). It's accurate (the `RBRN` runtime field does have a `podman` value) and it still reads as "deferred → future," so I'd leave it. Flag only if you want *zero* whiff of partial presence; then we'd drop "architecturally accommodated by the spec" and just say "deferred."

2. **The real loose end is outside the README.** Your call cleanly resolves the *README* half of the contradiction I flagged — but the *spec* half is untouched: `RBSHR` still says *"Recipe Bottle currently deploys to Podman on workstations… functional but not hardened,"* and `RBS0` still carries the live `RBRM` Podman-VM regime. So the document *set* stays self-contradictory; the README now says "future," the internal specs still say "present." That's veiled/internal, not public, and outside the README scope we've been working — but if you want the docs consistent, `RBSHR` needs its own pass.

So: **a full answer for the README (no edit needed); not a full answer for the doc set** (RBSHR/RBS0 still tell the present-tense Podman story). Want me to leave it entirely, do the optional one-phrase tightening on line 42, or note the RBSHR fix as a separate follow-up for another session?
